name: Al-Andalus
note: The Muslim-ruled territory of Iberia from the Umayyad conquest (711) to the fall of Granada (1492). At its peak (8th–10th centuries) Al-Andalus was the most sophisticated civilisation in Europe — Córdoba rivalled Constantinople and Baghdad. The Caliphate of Córdoba (929–1031) under Abd ar-Rahman III was the golden age. After 1031 it fragmented into taifa kingdoms; the Almoravid (1086) and Almohad (1147) Berber dynasties revived Muslim power but with more rigid Islamic orthodoxy. The Christian Reconquista progressively reduced Al-Andalus until only Granada remained (1238–1492). Concurrent with Christian kingdoms in the north throughout.

figures.name: Tariq ibn Ziyad
figures.significance: Led the Umayyad invasion of Iberia (711); crossed from Africa to Gibraltar (Jabal Tariq — 'Mountain of Tariq'); his 7,000-man army destroyed the Visigothic kingdom in a single battle

figures.name: Abd al-Rahman III
figures.significance: Declared Caliph of Córdoba (929); presided over the golden age of Al-Andalus; Córdoba under his rule was the most sophisticated city in Europe

figures.name: Averroes (Ibn Rushd)
figures.significance: The greatest Islamic philosopher; his commentaries on Aristotle transmitted classical Greek philosophy to medieval Europe through Latin translations — sparked the European scholastic tradition

figures.name: Muhammad XII (Boabdil)
figures.significance: Last ruler of Granada; surrendered the Alhambra to Ferdinand and Isabella on January 2, 1492 — the last Muslim ruler in Iberia. Legend: he wept as he left, and his mother said 'Weep like a woman for what you could not defend as a man'
